column1: What do SPI and I2C have in common?;Both are synchronous short-range buses (a clock line tells the receiver when to sample
column3: both are built into every STM32
column4: and choosing between them is a routine design decision.

column1: What are SPI's four wires?;SCLK (clock from the controller)
How does SPI select a device and exchange data?;No addressing and no acknowledgement — the controller pulls a device's CS low
column2: then clocks bits. Internally two shift registers form a ring
column3: so each clock edge sends a bit on MOSI and receives one on MISO — a transfer is inherently an exchange (to read

column1: What are CPOL and CPHA in SPI?;Clock Polarity (idle clock high or low) and Clock Phase (sample on the first or second edge). They define the four SPI modes (0-3)
column2: and controller and peripheral must agree or you read garbage — a mismatched mode is the classic SPI returns 0x00 or 0xFF bring-up bug.

column1: What are I2C's two wires?;SDA (serial data
column3: driven by the controller) — shared by every device on the bus.

column1: Why are I2C lines open-drain with pull-ups?;A device can only pull a line low or release it to float high
column2: which lets many devices share the wires without conflict and enables acknowledgement and clock stretching.

column1: How does I2C select a device?;There is no chip-select line
column2: so each peripheral has a 7-bit address
column3: and the controller broadcasts the address and only the matching device responds.

column1: How is an I2C transaction framed?;START (SDA falls while SCL is high)
column2: then 7 address bits + 1 R/W direction bit
column3: an ACK/NACK after each byte (the receiver pulls SDA low to ACK)
column5: then STOP (SDA rises while SCL is high).

column1: What is I2C clock stretching?;A peripheral that needs more time holds SCL low
column2: pausing the controller until it is ready.

column5: tens of MHz. I2C = 2 wires total

column3: pull-ups.;SPI selects by a dedicated chip-select line
column5: and needs no pull-ups. I2C selects by a 7-bit address on the bus

column1: When choose SPI versus I2C?;SPI for raw speed and full-duplex streaming (SD cards
column4: accepting an extra wire per device. I2C when pins are scarce and you have many slow-ish devices
column5: accepting lower speed and a fussier protocol.

column1: How do SPI/I2C appear inside the STM32?;Just more memory-mapped peripherals: control registers for mode/speed
column5: and a data register. They support interrupts and DMA
column7: HAL_I2C_Mem_Read) and LL for tight ISRs.

column1: What is the STM32 HAL I2C address-shift gotcha?;The HAL wants the 7-bit address shifted left by one (leaving room for the R/W bit)
column2: so a device at 0x50 is passed as 0xA0 — a perennial source of device not responding confusion.

column1: Why is connecting eight identical parts a problem on I2C
column2: and how is it solved?;I2C selects devices by address
column3: so eight identical parts share one default address and collide. Solved with parts that expose address-select pins (strap each to a unique address)
